---
title: "SHAP"
format: html
editor: visual
---

# Learning Objectives

Our learning objectives are to:

-   Understand the XGBoost algorithm using explanable AI SHAP

-   Interpret and explain the various plots from SHAP to do a local and global interpretation

# ML Workflow with XGBoost

## Step 1: Load Libraries

We begin by loading the required libraries:

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#install.packages("xgboost") #new pacakage
#install.packages("shapviz")
library(tidymodels)   # Core framework for modeling (includes recipes, workflows, parsnip, etc.)
library(finetune)     # Additional tuning strategies (e.g., racing, ANOVA-based tuning)
library(vip)          # For plotting variable importance from fitted models
library(xgboost)      # XGBoost implementation in R
library(ranger)       # Fast implementation of Random Forests
library(tidyverse)    # Data wrangling and visualization
library(doParallel)   # For parallel computing (useful during resampling/tuning)
library(shapviz)
#library(caret)       # Other great library for Machine Learning 
```

# Load the data set

-   Setting a seed ensures reproducibility.
-   Splitting data helps validate generalizability by preventing the model from seeing the test data during training.

```{r weather}
weather <- read_csv("../data/weather_monthsum.csv")
weather
```

-   We begin by splitting the dataset into **training** and **testing** sets.
-   We use **stratified sampling** to ensure that the distribution of the target variable is maintained in both sets.
-   A random **seed** is set to ensure reproducibility of the split.
-   This step ensures that the model is validated on unseen data, helping assess its **generalizability**.

> ⚠️ Note: Since tree-based models (like XGBoost and Random Forest) are not affected by the scale of the features, **normalization or standardization is not necessary**

# ML workflow

## 1. Pre-processing

```{r weather_split}
set.seed(931735) # Setting seed to get reproducible results 

weather_split <- initial_split(
  weather, 
  prop = .7, # proption of split same as previous codes
  strata = strength_gtex  # Stratify by target variable
  )
weather_split
```

### a. Data split

For data split, let's use **70% training / 30% testing**.

```{r weather_train}
weather_train <- training(weather_split)  # 70% of data
weather_train #This is your traing data frame
```

```{r weather_test}
weather_test <- testing(weather_split)    # 30% of data
weather_test
```

### b. Distribution of target variable

```{r distribution}
ggplot() +
  geom_density(data = weather_train, 
               aes(x = strength_gtex),
               color = "red") +
  geom_density(data = weather_test, 
               aes(x = strength_gtex),
               color = "blue") 
  
```

### c. Data processing with recipe

Before training, we need to perform some processing steps, like

-   normalizing
-   **removing unimportant variables**
-   dropping NAs
-   performing PCA on the go
-   removing columns with single value
-   others?

For that, we'll create a **recipe** of these processing steps.

This recipe will then be applied now to the **train data**, and easily applied to the **test data** when we bring it back at the end.

Creating a recipe is an easy way to port your processing steps for other data sets without needing to repeat code, and also only considering the data it is being applied to.

You can find all available recipe step options here: https://tidymodels.github.io/recipes/reference/index.html

Different model types require different processing steps.\
Let's check what steps are required for an elastic net model (linear_reg). We can search for that in this link: https://www.tmwr.org/pre-proc-table #Don't need for numeric data

> Differently from elastic net, variables do not need to be normalized in XgBoost, so we'll skip this step.

```{r weather_recipe}
# Create recipe for data preprocessing
weather_recipe <- recipe(strength_gtex ~ ., data = weather_train) %>% # Remove identifier columns and months not in growing season
  step_rm(
    year,
    site,
    matches("Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|Nov|Dec")  # Remove non-growing season months
  )

weather_recipe
```

```{r weather_prep}
# Prep the recipe to estimate any required statistics
weather_prep <- weather_recipe %>% 
  prep()

# Examine preprocessing steps
weather_prep
```


## 5. Final Specification

```{r final_spec_fit}
final_spec <- boost_tree(
  trees = 1927,           # Number of boosting rounds (trees)
  tree_depth = 13, # Maximum depth of each tree
  min_n = 27,           # Minimum number of samples to split a node
  learn_rate = 0.00067  # Learning rate (step size shrinkage)
) %>%
  set_engine("xgboost") %>%
  set_mode("regression")

final_spec
```

## 6. Final Fit and Predictions

## Validation

```{r final_fit}
set.seed(10)
final_fit <- last_fit(final_spec,
                weather_recipe,
                split = weather_split)

final_fit %>%
  collect_predictions()
```

## 7. Evaluate on Test Set

```{r final_fit_metrics}
final_fit %>%
  collect_metrics()
```

-R2 & RMSE = 0.2049.

-best_r2_pct_loss = 0.2049.

-best_r2_one_std_error = 0.1936.

-best_rmse_pct_loss = 0.1855.

-best_rmse_one_std_err = 0.1254.

## 8. Evaluate on Training Set

```{r}
final_spec %>%
  fit(strength_gtex ~ .,
      data = bake(weather_prep, 
                  weather_train)) %>%
  augment(new_data = bake(weather_prep, 
                          weather_train)) %>% 
  rmse(strength_gtex, .pred) %>%
  bind_rows(
    
    
# R2
final_spec %>%
  fit(strength_gtex ~ .,
      data = bake(weather_prep, 
                  weather_train)) %>%
  augment(new_data = bake(weather_prep, 
                          weather_train)) %>% 
  rsq(strength_gtex, .pred))
```

## 9. Predicted vs Observed Plot

```{r}
final_fit %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = strength_gtex,
             y = .pred)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(20, 40)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(20, 40)) 
```

## 10. Variable Importance

```{r final_spec}
final_spec %>%
  fit(strength_gtex ~ .,
         data = bake(weather_prep, weather_train)) %>% #There little change in variable improtance if you use full dataset
    vi() %>%
  mutate(
    Variable = fct_reorder(Variable, 
                           Importance)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Importance, 
             y = Variable)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(y = NULL)
```

```{r}
# To visualize the SHAP we need 2 things, model fit and the data (test or train) in a matrix format

# SHAP ANALYSIS with your final_fit (last_fit)

## 1. Prepare the pre-processed test data (exactly as the model saw it)
test_baked <- 

## 2. Create the matrix for SHAP computation (predictors only)
X_pred <- test_baked %>% 


## 3. Extract the raw xgboost model from your last_fit object
xgb_fitted <- 

## 4. Compute SHAP values (TreeSHAP – very fast)
shap_obj <- shapviz(

)

print(shap_obj)   # Quick check that it worked
```

### Interpretation of the SHAP Summary (Beeswarm) Plot

The **SHAP summary plot** (also called beeswarm plot) provides a compact, information-rich overview of how each feature influences the model's predictions for `strength_gtex` across the entire **test set**. It combines **feature importance** with the **direction and magnitude** of each feature's effect.

#### How to Read This Plot

-   **Y-axis (vertical)**: Features are ranked from most to least important (based on the mean absolute SHAP value). The top feature has the largest overall impact on predicted `strength_gtex`.
-   **X-axis (horizontal)**: SHAP value.
    -   Positive values (right of 0) → the feature **increases** the predicted `strength_gtex`.
    -   Negative values (left of 0) → the feature **decreases** the predicted `strength_gtex`.
-   **Each dot** represents one observation (test sample). Dots that pile up vertically show higher density (more common values).
-   **Color** represents the original feature value:
    -   **Yellow/Orange** = high value of that variable
    -   **Purple** = low value of that variable

#### Key Insights from Your Plot

1.  **Most Important Features**\
    The top three variables have the strongest influence:

    -   `mean_dayl_s_Sep` (mean day length in September) — mean \|SHAP\| = 0.352
    -   `mean_tmin_c_Sep` (mean minimum temperature in September) — 0.301
    -   `mean_srad_wm2_Aug` (mean solar radiation in August) — 0.284

    These late-summer / early-fall weather conditions are the strongest drivers of predicted grass strength in your model.

2.  **Direction of Effects** (Relationship Patterns)

    -   For **September day length** (`mean_dayl_s_Sep`): High values (yellow) mostly push SHAP values positive → longer day length in September **increases** predicted `strength_gtex`.
    -   For **September minimum temperature** (`mean_tmin_c_Sep`): High values (yellow) tend to appear on the positive side → warmer minimum temperatures in September are associated with **higher** strength.
    -   For **August solar radiation** (`mean_srad_wm2_Aug`): High values (yellow) are mostly on the positive side, while low values (purple) are more on the negative side → higher solar radiation in August strongly **increases** the prediction.
    -   Similar patterns appear for other temperature and radiation variables in May–October (growing season months).

3.  **Overall Pattern**\
    Most of the top features show a clear trend: **higher values** (yellow) tend to produce **positive SHAP values** (increase the prediction), while **lower values** (purple) produce **negative SHAP values** (decrease the prediction). This suggests that warmer temperatures, longer days, and higher solar radiation during the growing season are beneficial for `strength_gtex`.

#### What This Means in Context

Your XGBoost model has learned that late-summer and early-fall weather conditions (especially in September) are critical for grass strength. Variables related to temperature and solar radiation dominate over precipitation in this dataset. The spread of dots shows variability — the effect is not perfectly linear, and some observations show stronger or weaker influences depending on the exact conditions.

This global view complements the variable importance plot you generated earlier with `vip()` and helps validate that the model is capturing biologically meaningful relationships.

```{r}
## === GLOBAL SHAP PLOTS ===

# 1. SHAP Summary Beeswarm Plot (most informative – shows importance + effect direction)
sv_importance(shap_obj,) +
  

# 2. SHAP Bar Importance Plot
sv_importance(shap_obj,) +
  ggtitle("SHAP Global Feature Importance (Mean |SHAP Value|)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

### SHAP Dependence Plot: Interaction Between May Temperature and September Day Length

This **SHAP dependence plot** shows how the value of one feature (`mean_dayl_s_Sep` – mean day length in September) affects the model’s prediction of `strength_gtex`, while also revealing **interactions** with another feature (here colored by `mean_dayl.s_Aug` – mean day length in August).

#### How to Read a SHAP Dependence Plot

-   **X-axis**: The actual value of the feature being examined (`mean_dayl_s_Sep` in this case).
-   **Y-axis**: The SHAP value for that feature — i.e., how much this feature contributes to pushing the prediction **higher** (positive) or **lower** (negative).
-   **Each point** represents one observation from the test set.
-   **Color** indicates the value of a second interacting feature (in this plot: August day length). Yellow = high values, purple = low values.

#### Key Insights from This Plot

-   There is a **strong positive relationship** between September day length and its SHAP contribution.
    -   When September day length is shorter (around 43,600–43,720 seconds), the SHAP values are mostly **negative** (ranging from -1.0 to -0.3). This means short September days **decrease** the predicted grass strength.
    -   As September day length increases (moving right on the x-axis), the SHAP values rise sharply and become **positive** (up to +0.6). Longer days in September strongly **increase** the predicted `strength_gtex`.
-   The plot also reveals an **interaction effect** with August day length:
    -   Points with **higher August day length** (yellow/orange) tend to sit higher on the plot, especially at longer September day lengths.
    -   This suggests that the beneficial effect of long September days is **amplified** when August also had longer days. In other words, consecutive months with longer daylight hours have a synergistic positive impact on grass strength.
-   The relationship is roughly **non-linear** — the positive effect becomes stronger once September day length exceeds approximately 43,750 seconds.

#### Biological Interpretation

This dependence plot supports the idea that **day length during late summer and early fall** is critical for grass strength. Longer photoperiods in September likely allow more time for photosynthesis and carbohydrate accumulation before winter, leading to stronger turf. The interaction with August day length indicates that consistent long days across the peak growing season further enhance this effect.

This visualization goes beyond simple variable importance by showing **how** and **under what conditions** a feature influences the prediction.

```{r}
## === DEPENDENCE PLOTS (how individual features affect predictions) ===

# Check your available variable names first
colnames(X)

# Example dependence plot – replace "May_temp" with one of your actual column names
sv_dependence(shap_obj, ) +   # change variable name
  ggtitle("SHAP Dependence Plot: Sep_temp") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### SHAP Waterfall Plot for Test Observation #1

The **SHAP Waterfall Plot** explains **exactly why** the XGBoost model made a specific prediction for this particular observation (Test row #1). It shows how each feature pushes the prediction up or down from the average (baseline) prediction.

#### How to Read This Waterfall Plot

-   **Bottom bar (E\[f(x)\] = 30.4)**: This is the **baseline prediction** — the average predicted `strength_gtex` across the entire dataset (what the model would predict if it knew nothing about this specific observation).
-   **Top bar (f(x) = 27.2)**: This is the **final prediction** for this specific test observation.
-   **Each bar in between** represents one feature’s contribution (its SHAP value):
    -   **Red bars** (pointing left) → The feature **decreases** the prediction.
    -   **Yellow bars** (pointing right) → The feature **increases** the prediction.
    -   The length of the bar shows the **magnitude** of the effect.
-   The plot starts at the baseline (30.4) and **adds/subtracts** each feature’s contribution step by step until it reaches the final prediction (27.2).

#### Key Insights from This Observation

For this particular test sample: - The strongest negative driver is **`mean_dayl_s_Sep = 43693`** (very short September day length), which **decreased** the predicted strength by **0.912** units — the largest single push downward. - Other negative contributors include low solar radiation in July (`mean_srad_wm2_Jul = 405`), lower September maximum temperature, and low August solar radiation. - Only one feature had a noticeable positive effect: **`mean_tmin_c_Oct = 9`** (yellow bar +0.211), which slightly increased the prediction. - The remaining **27 other features** had very small individual effects, so they are grouped together at the bottom.

Overall, the model predicted **27.2** for this observation (lower than the average of 30.4), mainly because of **suboptimal late-summer and early-fall conditions** — especially the short day length in September.

This local explanation helps us understand the model’s decision for one specific case, which is very useful when you want to interpret individual predictions (e.g., why a particular month/year had low grass strength).

```{r}
## === INDIVIDUAL PREDICTION EXPLANATIONS ===

# Waterfall plot for the same observation (very clear breakdown)
sv_waterfall(shap_obj, ) +
  ggtitle("SHAP Waterfall Plot - Test Observation #1")
```

## Summary

In this exercise, we covered: -XGBoost algorithm.

-   Interpret the post-hoc models like XGBoost using Explainable AI like SHAP

-   Visualize and Interpret the results from the SHAP,

# Further Resources

-   A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions {Scott M Lundberg, Su-In Lee Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 30 (NIPS 2017)}
